metric_value: Arrived at DEC in late 1987 carrying intimate technical knowledge from six years at Stratus and a freshly completed ghost-written Yankee Group report.
Title was Marketing Executive — DEC's highest-level individual contributor in marketing.
Assignment from Bill Steul's Corporate Systems group: 'look around DEC for things that needed to be done to improve the enterprise position.'
Online Transaction Processing was 'still an IBM mainframe franchise' for banking, insurance, retail, and financial services as of 1987.
DEC had the VAX architecture, a genuine relational database in Rdb, and a transaction monitor — but lacked a verifiable competitive story.
'The Future of Transaction Processing' published by Yankee Group in January 1987.
source_page: The Study Nobody Knew I Had Written

metric_name: Ghost-writing fee for Yankee Group report
metric_value: Hired by John Logan at Yankee Group for $8,000 and a month of late evenings to ghostwrite 'The Future of Transaction Processing.'
source_page: The Study Nobody Knew I Had Written

metric_value: Former Prime Computer colleague who moved to Yankee Group; commissioned Kastner's ghost-written report on transaction processing.
source_page: The Study Nobody Knew I Had Written

metric_value: Moore's Law was meeting economics of departmental transaction processing; multiprocessor Intel 386 systems were appearing; mainframe cost-per-transaction advantage was evaporating.
source_page: The Study Nobody Knew I Had Written

metric_value: Mainframe displacement was 'not a matter of whether but when — and when was the early 1990s.'
source_page: The Study Nobody Knew I Had Written

metric_value: DECtp launch was eight months out when Kastner arrived at DEC; he became apparent Chair of the DECtp Task Force.
source_page: The Study Nobody Knew I Had Written

metric_value: Kastner networked furiously across ten DEC product groups to build the DECtp story; title of Task Force Chair never received in writing.
source_page: The Study Nobody Knew I Had Written

metric_value: DEC's hand was stronger than team realized: multiprocessor VAX 8200 + transaction-friendly Rdb + acquired OLTP transaction monitor.
source_page: The Study Nobody Knew I Had Written

metric_value: The SNA Tax: hidden overhead in IBM's mainframe architecture costing enterprise customers millions of dollars they couldn't see.
source_page: The Study Nobody Knew I Had Written

metric_value: DECtp marketing launch budget (excluding product R&D) totaled approximately $2 million — described as 'staggering' for a marketing program in 1988.
notes: Kastner states this was 'not previously disclosed.'

metric_value: Blind benchmark conducted through a CICS consulting firm in Westwood, Massachusetts using the Cullinet mainframe data center.
John Cullinane, known to Kastner from PHI days, 'had built one of the first major commercial software companies.' The Route 128 world was always smaller than it appeared.
Objective: measure IBM's highest possible throughput on a 3090 running DB2, then compare head-to-head against a DEC VAX under identical constraints.
IBM's own systems analysts brought in under contract; told in writing IBM was to provide all reasonable services and tuning to maximize throughput; unaware DEC was the client.
Simulates bank account transaction processing; measures TPS with 100 virtual terminals per claimed 1 TPS; requires 10,000 virtual users for 100 TPS claim; response time under one second.
source_page: Sidebar: What Is the Debit-Credit Benchmark?

metric_value: Called the 'four-minute mile' of enterprise computing; every serious OLTP vendor obsessed with it; single test score could define competitive position for years.
source_page: Sidebar: What Is the Debit-Credit Benchmark?

metric_value: Kastner had extensive debit-credit benchmark experience from Stratus vs. Tandem competitive engagements.
source_page: Sidebar: What Is the Debit-Credit Benchmark?

metric_value: IBM 3090-400 as System Under Test; IBM 3090-200 as load driver — two of IBM's largest mainframes in the same room, running tests at night and on weekends.

metric_name: SNA Tax: VTAM session initialization cost
metric_value: More than one full 3090 CPU-second required to log in a single 3270 terminal via VTAM.

metric_name: SNA Tax: total session overhead for 100 TPS
metric_value: 100 TPS benchmark required 10,000 virtual terminals × 1 CPU-second = 10,000 CPU-seconds of session overhead; over an hour of clock time on a 4-processor 3090-400 before a single transaction.

metric_name: DB2 optimized for query, not transactions
metric_value: DB2 had been tuned almost exclusively for query workloads; Edgar Codd and Christopher Date designed the relational model for data retrieval; transactional throughput was 'an architectural afterthought.'
Edgar Codd and Christopher Date are credited as the IBM researchers who invented the relational database model; designed for elegant retrieval, not transactional throughput.
DEC head of Competitive Analysis; used an HP color graphing plotter to produce presentation foils from Kastner's benchmark data in under two hours.
VAX running OLTP database with clean transaction architecture delivered comparable throughput to IBM 3090 at a small fraction of the cost.

Presented to industry analysts in Boston and press corps in New York; used one color slide showing millions of dollars price difference between IBM 3090-400 and DEC VAX with almost no performance difference.
source_page: The Press Event and the Slide That Changed the Room

metric_value: First reaction in the room: 'That's a sleazy, cheating way for DEC to act.' Kastner held questions to the end; presentation had been built to preempt the objection. Press and analysts accepted it.
source_page: The Press Event and the Slide That Changed the Room

metric_value: Sales improved measurably following the press and analyst presentation of the DEC vs. IBM benchmark results.
source_page: The Press Event and the Slide That Changed the Room

metric_value: In October 1988, IBM rushed out a press announcement claiming DB2 now ran much faster than DEC's measurements; DB2 Version 2 announcement was an acknowledgment DEC had found real weaknesses.
source_page: The Press Event and the Slide That Changed the Room

metric_value: By 1988 OLTP benchmark world had degenerated into 'specsmanship' — vendors designed their own tests, optimized every parameter, published results unreproducible in real deployments.
source_page: Sidebar: Specsmanship and Why It Had to End

metric_value: A vendor could claim 500 TPS by selecting workloads that favored their architecture, excluding real-deployment overhead; every vendor won its own benchmark; customers had no trustworthy comparison basis.
source_page: Sidebar: Specsmanship and Why It Had to End

metric_value: Omri Serlin, a respected OLTP analyst, had been pushing for independent audited industry-standard benchmarks for years; industry was hesitant; result was paralysis.

Over dinner with Omri Serlin in Los Altos, California: 'Digital Equipment would be pleased to be the first member of the Transaction Processing Council. Let's make some phone calls and get the rest off the fence.'

Kastner leaked Digital's TPC leadership position to MIS Week on August 8, 1988; once DEC's commitment was public, other vendors faced a binary choice: join or explain refusal to compete on a level field.
TPC required full system pricing (hardware + software + maintenance), independent certified audit, and full public disclosure of configuration and methodology.
source_page: Sidebar: The TPC and Its Lasting Impact

metric_value: TPC-C became the definitive OLTP server benchmark through the 1990s and into the 2000s; results published in trade press quarterly and tracked like stock prices in the server market.
source_page: Sidebar: The TPC and Its Lasting Impact

metric_value: TPC transformed the OLTP market: vendors could no longer claim self-serving results; CIOs had objective, auditable data; benchmark became standard in every enterprise server RFP for a decade.
source_page: Sidebar: The TPC and Its Lasting Impact

metric_value: Kastner realized his OLTP technical depth plus TPC methodology made him an accredited benchmark auditor; established Aberdeen Transaction Services as a practice area.
AICPA manual had a brief chapter on performance auditing; Kastner used it as procedural basis; 'the secret was mundane: methodical process and impeccable notes.'
Billing rate was $15,000 for a couple of days of careful documentation work per benchmark audit.
Kastner's Aberdeen Transaction Services audited Stratus's TPC-A benchmark submission.
Kastner audited the Stratus/IBM proprietary RAMP-C benchmark submission.
Kastner audited three models of Groupe Bull DPS 7000 and 6000 systems across the United States, Italy, and France.

Kastner conducted one audit session staying at the Trianon Palace Versailles, reviewing benchmark logs at a desk overlooking the palace gardens while engineers wrestled with communications configurations.
Auditing practice ended with TPC-C, when council began requiring certified auditors with formal examinations under authorized firms. Described as 'a productive and pleasantly remunerative sideline.'
DEC's senior marketing executive Jack Smith later told peers: 'DECtp was our most important launch after VAX.'
VAX architecture was 'magnificent — one of the great minicomputer designs ever produced.' But the battle DEC was winning (mini vs. mainframe) was not the defining war of the 1990s.
The war that would define the 1990s was x86-versus-everything-else; DEC beat IBM's mainframe pricing in OLTP; within four years x86 servers would beat DEC's VAX pricing in the same argument.
By 1990, Compaq and others were building x86 servers delivering competitive OLTP throughput at a fraction of DEC VAX pricing.

metric_name: Ken Olsen's resistance to x86 and Unix
metric_value: Ken Olsen, DEC's founder, famously resisted Unix, workstations, and x86 architecture throughout the late 1980s and early 1990s.

metric_name: DEC displaced by TPC benchmarks it helped create
metric_value: DEC, which showed the industry how to use objective benchmarks to displace the mainframe, was itself displaced by x86 vendors using those same TPC benchmarks against proprietary minicomputers.
notes: Described as 'irony almost too neat to be credible.'

metric_value: Left DEC for Aberdeen with the x86 displacement observation 'fully formed'; Aberdeen spent much of the next decade documenting the collapse of the proprietary minicomputer era.
'The mainframe had not been the last stand, it turned out. It was the second-to-last stand.' — referring to VAX/minicomputer era as the actual last stand before x86 dominance.
Kastner's Yankee Group report stated multiprocessor Intel 386 systems were 'appearing on the horizon'; by 1988 DECtp launch those systems were already arriving.
notes: Prediction proved accurate ahead of schedule.

metric_value: Discovery was described as 'devastating to IBM's narrative and priceless to ours' — the SNA Tax was the core competitive intelligence finding of the Westwood blind benchmark.

metric_name: Kastner's Stratus vs. Tandem benchmark experience
metric_value: Kastner gained extensive debit-credit benchmark experience during his six years at Stratus, competing against Tandem in OLTP fault-tolerant markets.
source_page: Sidebar: What Is the Debit-Credit Benchmark?

metric_value: Kastner left DEC for Aberdeen Group; Aberdeen's core advisory practice grew to the point where $15,000 benchmark audits were not the best use of time, ending the ATS sideline.
Six years at Stratus before DEC; provided 'intimate technical knowledge of fault-tolerant computing' brought to the DECtp effort.
IBM's DB2 Version 2 announcement (claiming faster performance) was described as crystallizing the need for independent standards: 'the era of proprietary benchmarks had to end.'
Kastner informed Bill Steul he was heading to Los Altos, California to meet Omri Serlin and launch the TPC founding process.

metric_name: OLTP vendor resistance to independent standards
metric_value: No vendor wanted to step into a ring where they couldn't control the rules; result before TPC founding was industry paralysis on benchmark standards.
Co-inventor of the relational database model with Edgar Codd at IBM; the relational model was designed for elegant data retrieval, not transactional throughput.
Strategy was: combine multiprocessor VAX 8200 + transaction-friendly Rdb + acquired OLTP transaction monitor; benchmark against IBM 3090; expose the SNA Tax.
source_page: The Study Nobody Knew I Had Written

metric_value: Kastner networked across ten DEC product groups to build the DECtp competitive story and launch.
source_page: The Study Nobody Knew I Had Written

metric_name: SNA Tax as hidden enterprise cost
metric_value: The SNA Tax was the hidden overhead buried in IBM's mainframe architecture 'costing enterprise customers millions of dollars they couldn’t see.'
source_page: The Study Nobody Knew I Had Written

metric_value: IBM analysts knew a benchmark was being run and had written instructions to maximize throughput, but had no idea DEC was the client behind the curtain.

Once DEC's TPC commitment was public (August 8, 1988 MIS Week), every other serious OLTP vendor faced binary choice — join the standard or explain to customers why they refused. 'The fence cleared quickly.'

metric_name: Moore's Law meeting transaction processing economics
metric_value: Moore's Law was 'finally meeting the economics of departmental transaction processing' by 1987, making Intel 386-based multiprocessor servers viable for OLTP.
source_page: The Study Nobody Knew I Had Written

metric_value: Aberdeen spent much of the next decade documenting 'the collapse of the proprietary minicomputer era and the rise of the open, standards-based, x86-dominated enterprise infrastructure.'

metric_name: x86 vendors use TPC-C to displace minicomputers
metric_value: Compaq and other x86 server vendors used TPC benchmarks (created with DEC's leadership) to displace DEC VAX and other proprietary minicomputer architectures — same methodology DEC used against IBM mainframe.

metric_name: Kastner's dual knowledge at DEC arrival
metric_value: Arrived at DEC carrying two undisclosed assets: six years of Stratus fault-tolerant computing expertise, and an $8,000 ghost-written Yankee Group study predicting mainframe economic collapse.
VTAM (IBM's mainframe communications manager) required more than one full 3090 CPU-second just to log in a single 3270 terminal session.
100 TPS requires 10,000 terminals; 10,000 × 1 CPU-second = 10,000 CPU-seconds of session overhead; on 4-processor 3090-400 = over an hour of clock time before first transaction.

metric_name: Richard Case reaction to benchmark data
metric_value: Richard Case, DEC head of Competitive Analysis, was 'ecstatic'; used HP color graphing plotter to produce cost-performance gap presentation foils in under two hours.

The DECtp benchmark press presentation used one color slide (showing the price-performance gap vs. IBM) among otherwise all-black-and-white materials. 'That single slide was worth every penny of the $2 million launch budget.'
source_page: The Press Event and the Slide That Changed the Room

metric_value: DECtp benchmark results presented to industry analysts in Boston and press corps in New York.
source_page: The Press Event and the Slide That Changed the Room

metric_value: Kastner walked through written instructions to IBM analysts, open invitation to tune DB2 to maximum, identical workload on both platforms, and auditable methodology.
source_page: The Press Event and the Slide That Changed the Room

metric_value: Kastner's approach: hold questions to the end; build presentation to anticipate the 'cheating' objection and answer it before anyone asks; ensures objection is pre-answered when questions come.
source_page: The Press Event and the Slide That Changed the Room

metric_value: Both Kastner and John Logan had come from Prime Computer before their respective moves to DEC and Yankee Group; Route 128 technology community was tightly interconnected.
source_page: The Study Nobody Knew I Had Written

metric_value: Kastner knew John Cullinane from his earlier PHI Computer Services days; illustrated the tight Route 128 professional network.
French and Italian Groupe Bull engineers were 'impressive in their technical tenacity and their frustration when terminal emulators misbehaved at critical moments.'
TPC-C era required 'certified' auditors with formal examinations operating under authorized firms; ended informal benchmark auditing practice.
DECtp campaign was a success: sales into OLTP accounts improved; TPC was founded; mainframe cost-performance advantage was documented, quantified, and published.

metric_name: IBM response timing to DEC benchmark
metric_value: IBM 'spent their own nights and weekends closing the gaps we had exposed' before announcing DB2 Version 2 in October 1988.
source_page: The Press Event and the Slide That Changed the Room

metric_value: OLTP was the 'heartbeat of banking, insurance, retail, and financial services' — the core enterprise computing workload dominated by IBM mainframe as of 1987.
Leak to MIS Week served a 'precise purpose': once DEC's commitment was public, every other serious OLTP vendor faced a binary choice — join the standard or explain refusal to customers.
TPC founding commitment made over dinner in Los Altos, California between Kastner and Omri Serlin.
Digital Equipment Corporation was the first member of the Transaction Processing Council, announced publicly via MIS Week August 8, 1988.
